speaker: interviewer
text: December 18, 1996. Interview with Aron Bell, born Bielsky. My name is Lillian Gewirtzman. New York, New York, USA. The language is English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December 18, 1996. Interview with Aron Bell, born Bielsky. My name is Lillian Gewirtzman, G-E-W-I-R-T-Z-M-A-N. New York, New York, USA. The language is English. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Good morning, Mr. Bell. Would you please tell us your full name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aron Bell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A-R-O-N B-E-L-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a different name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: First is the same, Aron. The second was Bielsky, B-I-E-L-S-K-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell us the name of the town and country where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I see this is gonna be pretty hard on me. I can see it right now. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was born near a village which had called Stankiewicze. The closest town was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 15 kilometers away, which the name was Nowogrodek.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: State of White Russia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which-- which it is now-- they call it Belarus. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In their language, the White Russian language, they call it Belarusia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] January, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does it make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old does it make me now? What are we now, in '96? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Same place where we were all born-- four generations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] According to my older brothers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three or four generations in Stankiewicze.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a mill [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the villagers would come with grain and you make all kind of cereals and flour. And we had a farm. Whatever a farm has, that's what we had, whether it's a turkey or chicken or duck or a goose, horses, cows-- regular farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the farm just for your own consumption, or did you sell some of the products?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some for our own consumption, and some they used to sell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bella.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] [SOBS] Her maiden name was Mendelewicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: A village-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] [CRYING] a village called Stankiewicze. I mean, [? Petrovich, ?] forgive me, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like 10 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were there in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were 10 brothers and two sisters-- 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell me about them, their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm not going to go through all of them. It's really hard, as I said before, to relive the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING] it's very hard for me, anyway, as I said, to relive that. I'll tell you some of them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING] As I said, I come from a farm. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We were all born and brought up there. I was the youngest out of 12. By the time I was born, the oldest was already in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Walter Bell. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Compared to other Jewish farmers, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the tough Jews in the area. What I mean by that is I never experienced, as far as I can remember from six, seven years of age-- I never experienced any antisemitism in any way or manner, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because most of my brothers were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six foot over tall, and they were tough men. [BACKGROUND NOISE] If any-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] if any the neighbors or anybody who ever knew them would never start up with the Bielsky family. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SIGHS] We're living a normal farmer's life. My parents and some of my brothers that remained in the house [INAUDIBLE] were Asael and Zisl. They worked on the farm or worked in the mill in order to support a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were some of your other siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of my brothers was taken by my uncle. He was ultra-religious, Orthodox. So he wanted that at least one should grow up a Orthodox-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he should be Orthodox, should practice religion. His name was Joshua Bielsky, and he became later on a rabbi. At 22, he got [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowogrodek. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] We were living like-- I guess it's known to the world-- like farmers live [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up until the 22nd of June, 1941, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when the Nazis occupied the whole area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we go back for a minute before the Nazis and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Be my guest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us about some others of your brothers that were still in Poland or in Belarus at the time, besides Zisl and Asael?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was an older-- Walter was already-- I'm-- what I am giving you the information is what I remember. At those days, Tuvia was somewhere else, like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 300 kilometers away from our farm. He was married and living a-- as a merchant. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I had another brother, Nathan, which was in America later on. He went in the very low '30s, came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did these two brothers leave to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: A good question, which I don't know if I could answer. I-- I asked later on, why didn't my parents leave? So the answer was it was very hard to get a visa to the United States, and financial hardship. It wasn't so easy to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] leave to another country. But after those two fellows-- Walter and Nathan, who had an uncle here in the United States, and he sent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a girl to pick them up, in other words, one at a time. And this is the way those two brothers were lucky enough to get to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean he sent a girl to pick them up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said he sent a girl to pick them up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, got married there, and this is the way-- later on-- that was before my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: These brothers were much older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in 1930, and the oldest was born in 1904. So I should say so. And the other one, Nathan, was born in 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which other siblings lived at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] At home lived another one, Abraham, Yankel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Asael. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: They were before my time. You have to understand that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is still alive, in Florida. Her name is Estelle Eshke. The other one, Toby, passed away about a year and a half ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they when you were still living at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Estelle was home, and Toby was married to another Jewish farmer, 10 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody else living on your farm with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had some help. We had a woman that lived all her life with us and then a-- a kid, Gentile kid, grew up with us, and he worked on the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the woman's name? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maria. Maria was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the kid who grew up on your farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Adolph Stishok.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old a kid was he when you remember him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he still a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember him as a kid. I remember him as a grown man.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say he probably was in the 30s when I remember him, in the 30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his job?

speaker: interviewee
text: For my time, he took care of the mill, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he live, inside your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends personally?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, who did you play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: At 10 years old-- the neighbors kids. But mainly, I guess, from Nowogrodek, when I went to school for three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of a school was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular grammar school. Public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- were the kids all Jewish? Were they mixed? Were there non-Jewish kids in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed. Mixed, but mainly Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all of your friends Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get along with the non-Jewish kids at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I never-- maybe I was lucky. I never experienced any antisemitism in any way or manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a large extended family living in your village with your uncles, aunts, grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a large extended family, of course. We had uncles, cousins. But none of us lived with us. They only used to come to visit us on vacation time, July and August.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a village did you live in? What would you say the population was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Population was about 10 families. Obvious they were Gentile. All White Russian except one family was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other Jewish families beside you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The closest Jewish family was, like, 10 kilometers, 12 kilometers away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish and White Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the religious practices of the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and mother were religious. They would not travel on the Sabbath. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They ate kosher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were traditional [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jews, except, as I mentioned before, one grew up an ultra Orthodox rabbi, which he died about 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any holiday was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about a holiday at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was every Friday night. They would put a white-- I don't even know how to say it in English-- a white cloth to cover the table. And-- and [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] my mother [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] would light candles. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think we always had a salad or a holiday meat. It was somewhat different than the regular day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite pastime as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would go swimming. I would go fishing. I would see how people work on the farm, how people work at the mill. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was my pastime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any chores you used to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until the war-- well, no, actually, not until the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your home look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: A regular farmer's big family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little house, it would call in our terms, in today's terms. We had one living room and one bedroom and a big kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We were not rich, obvious, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but we always had enough to live on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only different from our house on the farm would be-- and a Gentile family would be that it's-- the table would be covered and maybe a challah would lay on the white cloth, and candles would be lit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your immediate neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You mean you want the names of neighbors?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything you remember about them. I'm aware you were a young child. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember all of them well. Well, one of the neighbors saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Two brothers and a sister. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Their name was-- the younger one was Kostas, the other one was Mikhas, and their sister was Anzhia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] None of them are alive. Only one of those brother's wife is alive, which I visited them a couple of years back. Those are the people that actually saved my life, in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else in your immediate neighborhood that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember all of them. I remember the good and the bad. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So they were actually very good. They were the best, I guess, of the neighbors. But all of our neighbors were good people, except one, the Polish family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess, as far as I'm concerned, they were bad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Their name was Kuszel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the war started, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody was home except me and my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My two brothers-- those main two brothers that I will talk about are Asael and Zisl. They were in the Russian army. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was actually the only one, as far as the kids go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Abraham and Yankel-- after a week or so, they came also home from the city. After a week of what? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a week-- in a week after the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the start of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what I'm telling you now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- before the war, did your family have a radio? Did you get newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were they only one in the village that actually did have a radio, correct. And I remember people from all around the area, like 20 kilometers away, would come and listen to the radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was-- when we got the-- the radio-- I don't recall, really, whatever-- it was '39 or '40 or '41, one of those three years. Not in '41-- earlier. '39 or '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents listening to the news before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, talking politics with neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what they were talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Used to come, neighbors, and say to my father that he should take his family and escape, because a bad time is coming up in the state of White Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family know what was happening to the Jews in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew. But I don't think most of the population believed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those people. One of my brothers, Abraham-- he married to one of those girls that escaped from deep Poland, where it was occupied by the Nazis, I guess, in 1939. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was telling us stories. But then behind her back, I remember my mother, my father would not believe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the Nazis just random killing Jews. And we were not the only ones who did not believe. Others also-- other Jewish people-- did not trust or believe those few Jews that escaped from the Nazi-occupied Poland into state of White Russia. I hope I've made myself clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember them talking about Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they spoke about the Nazis, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember them being especially concerned about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me as a child?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your parents. Do you remember concern expressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much, really, but I wouldn't-- I couldn't answer that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't answer that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first experience the start of the war? When did you personally start experiencing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the war started the 22nd of June, 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And within two or three days, really, the Nazis dropped [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- what do you call? The parachute people, parachute soldiers, and the Nazis were even in our village. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is where-- from here I can tell you, for the next few years, our problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- what did you first see? When did you first see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did I first see them? I saw them, as I said, two or three days right after the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nazi soldiers coming with one Gentile policeman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Were coming to us, looking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for two brothers, Asael and Zisl. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was the very first time when I would see, personally, these soldiers or the policemen would come, I would run away and hide myself-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and hide myself. They were starting to come every day, and that's where the terror started in our house. It was hard. It is very hard. I don't think I'm intelligent enough to bring it across to you, the terror that we went through at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now.
